era_context: The Peloponnesian War enters its twentieth year. Athens, reeling from the catastrophic Sicilian Expedition (415–413 BCE), faces simultaneous threats from Sparta, its allies, and internal political instability. The oligarchic conspiracy that would culminate in the coup of 410 BCE is already in motion. Sparta, with increasing Persian financial support, is building a navy to challenge Athenian dominance. This is a year of profound crisis for the Athenian state.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (contemporary Chinese records exist but are not integrated)

events.title: Oligarchic conspiracy in Athens; planning of the Four Hundred coup
events.description: A secret oligarchic faction, including Antiphon, Phrynichus, Pisander, and others, began organizing a conspiracy to overthrow Athenian democracy. The conspirators exploited war fatigue, economic hardship, and the absence of the Athenian fleet (stationed at Samos) to plan a coup. They claimed that oligarchic rule would secure Persian financial support from Tissaphernes, though this promise was likely exaggerated or false. The conspiracy operated in secret societies (hetaireiai) and used intimidation against potential opponents. The coup would be executed in 410 BCE.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book VIII

events.certainty_note: Thucydides provides detailed account of the conspiracy's origins and organization. The dating to 411 BCE is based on Thucydides' narrative structure.

events.title: Athenian naval operations in the Hellespont and Aegean
events.description: The Athenian fleet, under commanders including Thrasybulus, conducted operations to maintain control of the Hellespont and secure grain supply routes from the Black Sea. These operations were crucial to Athens' survival, as the city depended on imported grain. The fleet also attempted to prevent Spartan-Corinthian naval expansion in the region. These campaigns were part of the broader strategy to maintain Athenian power despite the loss of resources and manpower from the Sicilian disaster.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book VIII

events.certainty_note: Thucydides documents these operations, though specific dates and details of individual engagements are sometimes unclear. The general pattern is well-established.
cross_references: Part of broader Peloponnesian War naval conflict

events.title: Spartan-Persian negotiations intensify; Lysander's rise
events.description: Sparta intensified diplomatic efforts with the Persian satrap Tissaphernes to secure financial support for naval construction. These negotiations were part of a broader Persian strategy to prevent Athenian hegemony while maintaining influence over Ionian Greek cities. The negotiations would eventually lead to the appointment of Lysander as Spartan commander, who would prove far more effective at securing Persian support than previous commanders. The Persian Empire's involvement was crucial to Sparta's ability to challenge Athenian naval dominance.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book VIII

events.certainty_note: Thucydides documents these negotiations, though the precise timing and details are sometimes unclear. Lysander's rise to prominence is better documented in later sources.
cross_references: Part of broader Persian involvement in Peloponnesian War

events.title: Alcibiades in exile; diplomatic contacts with Sparta and Persia
events.description: Alcibiades, the brilliant but controversial Athenian general, remained in exile following his recall from Sicily in 415 BCE. He maintained diplomatic contacts with both Sparta and Persia, offering his services and counsel to whichever power would benefit him most. Alcibiades was consulted by the oligarchic conspirators in Athens regarding Persian support, though his actual influence on events is debated. His presence as a potential alternative leader complicated Athenian politics and demonstrated the fragility of democratic institutions.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book VIII

events.certainty_note: Thucydides documents Alcibiades' diplomatic activities. The extent of his influence on the oligarchic conspiracy is debated by modern scholars.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative presents the oligarchic conspiracy as a unified, ideologically coherent movement, but modern scholarship has questioned this interpretation. The conspirators had diverse motivations: some were genuinely oligarchic in ideology, while others (like Theramenes) were more moderate and may have been coerced into participation. The promise of Persian support, which was central to the conspirators' pitch, is now viewed with skepticism by many scholars; Tissaphernes may have deliberately misled the conspirators. Additionally, the extent of Alcibiades' influence on the conspiracy is disputed; Thucydides may have overstated his role. Some modern scholars argue that the conspiracy was less a coherent political movement and more a series of opportunistic power grabs by competing factions.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 411 BCE is rich due to Thucydides' contemporary narrative, which covers this year in detail. However, Thucydides' account ends shortly after this year, and subsequent events are known primarily through later sources like Xenophon's Hellenica and Aristotle's Constitution of the Athenians. The perspective of Sparta, Corinth, and Persia is less directly represented. Thucydides' account may be biased toward the democratic perspective, though he attempts to present multiple viewpoints. The oligarchic conspirators left no contemporary written records, so their motivations and organization are known primarily through hostile sources.

graph_edges.note: The oligarchic conspiracy of 411 BCE was executed as the coup of 410 BCE
Athenian naval operations in 411 BCE set the stage for the victory at Cynossema in 410 BCE
Spartan-Persian negotiations intensified in 411 BCE and culminated in Cyrus the Younger's intervention in 407 BCE
Alcibiades' diplomatic activities were contemporary with the oligarchic conspiracy
Oligarchic conspiracy is part of broader Peloponnesian War conflict
